title: How to stay warm without turning up the heating
description: Practical ways to feel warmer without touching the thermostat: heat the person for pennies, stop draughts, and make the heating you pay for work harder.
blurb: Feel warmer for pennies an hour with person-heating tricks, draught fixes and smarter radiator habits, no thermostat required.
lede: Every degree you add on the thermostat heats the whole house: the hallway, the spare room, the cupboard under the stairs. Most of the time you do not need a warmer house, you need a warmer you, and warming a person costs a tiny fraction of warming a building. The trick is knowing which cheap fixes genuinely work, which gadgets cost pennies rather than pounds, and how to stop the heat you have already paid for leaking out through gaps and single glazing. This guide covers all three, with real running costs at current rates.
short_answer: Heating yourself instead of the house is the single biggest win: an electric blanket costs about 1.6p an hour and a heated throw about 3p, while raising the thermostat by one degree adds roughly £70 a year to a typical gas bill. Pair that with draught-proofing, closed curtains at dusk and well balanced radiators, and most people can sit comfortably at 18 to 19°C instead of 21°C. The savings run to hundreds of pounds over a winter.

sections.h2: Heat the person, not the building
sections.html:
Your boiler heats tens of thousands of litres of air, plus the walls, floors and furniture, just so the few cubic metres around your body feel pleasant. Anything that delivers warmth directly to you skips all that waste, which is why person-heating gadgets cost pennies per hour while central heating costs pounds.

The standouts:

  • Electric blanket or heated mattress topper. Around 60 watts, so about 1.6p an hour. Preheat the bed for 20 minutes and you climb into warmth for well under a penny. Our full electric blanket running cost guide has the detailed maths.
  • Heated throw. The sofa version of the electric blanket, typically 100 to 160 watts. Wrap one around you for an evening of telly and you have spent about 15p.
  • Hot water bottle. Boiling 1.5 litres in the kettle uses roughly 0.14 kWh, so a fill costs about 4p and the heat lasts a couple of hours. No standby, no cable, nothing to break.
  • Layers that actually insulate. A thermal base layer, a fleece or wool jumper, thick socks and slippers. Trapped air is what keeps you warm, so two thin layers beat one thick one. A blanket over the legs while sitting makes a surprising difference because seated bodies generate less heat.
  • Warm food and drink. A hot meal or a cup of tea raises how warm you feel for a fraction of a penny in kettle or hob energy.

One caution: cheap person-heating only stays cheap if it replaces thermostat degrees rather than adding to them. The point is to be comfortable at 18°C, not toasty at 21°C with a heated throw on top.

sections.h2: What each option costs per hour
sections.html:
Here is how the common keep-warm options compare on current electricity prices, including what a typical five hour evening costs for each.

OptionTypical powerCost per hourCost per 5 hour evening
Electric blanket (single)60W1.6p8p
Heated throw120W3.1p16p
Hot water bottle0.14 kWh per fillabout 4p per fill8p (two fills)
Small panel heater500W13p65p
Fan heater2,000W52p£2.61

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem cap July to September 2026. Hot water bottle assumes 1.5 litres boiled from cold in an electric kettle.

The pattern is clear. Gadgets that touch your body need very little power because they are not fighting the whole room. The 2kW fan heater sits at the bottom of the table for a reason: it is a room heater pretending to be a quick fix, and an evening of it costs more than 30 evenings under an electric blanket. If you are weighing up a heater for one room against running the boiler, our guide to heating one room versus the whole house works through that comparison properly.

For scale, the thermostat itself: turning it down by one degree cuts heating energy by roughly 10 per cent. On a typical home burning around 10,000 kWh of gas a year for heating, that is about 1,000 kWh saved, or roughly £70 a year at 7.33p per kWh. Drop from 21°C to 19°C and cover the difference with a £16-a-winter heated throw, and you are comfortably ahead.

sections.h2: Stop the heat you have already paid for escaping
sections.html:
Feeling cold at home is often not about the thermostat number at all. A room at 20°C with a draught blowing across it feels colder than a still room at 18°C, because moving air strips heat from your skin. Killing draughts is the cheapest comfort upgrade in the house, and most of it costs under a tenner per fix. Our quick wins under £10 hub collects the best of these.

  • Doors. Self-adhesive foam or brush strip around frames, a brush strip or escutcheon cover on the letterbox, a keyhole cover, and a draught excluder (a rolled towel works) along the bottom of doors. The full draught-proofing guide walks through each gap in order of payback.
  • Windows. Foam strip on opening frames, and clear secondary glazing film on single glazed windows. The film costs a few pounds per window and noticeably cuts the cold radiating off the glass.
  • Unused chimneys. An open flue can pull a small room's worth of warm air out of the house every hour. A chimney balloon or wool insert costs around £20 and fits in minutes. Leave a vent or fit a breathable type, and remove it before any fire is lit.
  • Curtains. Close them at dusk, every day. A decent lined curtain over a cold window can cut heat loss through the glass significantly, and thermal linings can be retrofitted cheaply. Just keep curtains tucked behind radiators, never draped over them, or you funnel your heat straight onto the glass.
  • Floors. Rugs on bare suspended timber floors block draughts rising between boards and make the room feel warmer underfoot, which matters more than air temperature for comfort.

None of this saves money by itself. It saves money because a draught-free room feels two or three degrees warmer than the thermostat says, which is what lets you turn the dial down without noticing.

sections.h2: Make the heating you do run work harder
sections.html:
If radiators are underperforming, you pay full price for partial heat, and the house never quite feels right. A few free checks fix the most common problems.

  • Bleed the radiators. A radiator that is cold at the top has trapped air and is running at reduced output for the same gas. Five minutes with a bleed key sorts it.
  • Unblock them. A sofa pushed against a radiator absorbs heat into its back panel instead of the room. Pull furniture 10 to 15 centimetres clear and stop using radiators as clothes dryers while a room feels cold, since drying laundry steals heat and pumps moisture into the air.
  • Fit reflectors behind radiators on external walls. Cheap foil reflector panels bounce heat back into the room instead of into the brickwork.
  • Turn the boiler flow temperature down. Counterintuitive but real: most combi boilers leave the factory set hotter than they need to be, and a lower flow temperature lets the boiler condense properly, squeezing more heat from every unit of gas. Our flow temperature guide shows the setting to aim for.
  • Use thermostatic radiator valves. Turn them down in rooms nobody uses during the day so the heat you generate goes where the people are.

If the heating runs for hours and the house still feels cold, something more specific is wrong, and it is usually one of the faults above or a balancing problem rather than a boiler on its way out.

sections.h2: Habits that work, and two that do not
sections.html:
A few behaviour changes cost nothing and stack up over a winter.

  • Move every hour. Ten minutes of housework, a walk up the stairs, anything. Muscle activity is your built-in heater, and sitting still for three hours is why evenings feel coldest.
  • Keep internal doors closed. Heat the rooms you live in and stop warm air wandering into the hallway. Smaller heated volume, faster warm-up, lower bills.
  • Deal with damp air. Humid air feels colder and takes more energy to heat. Lid on pans, bathroom door closed and window cracked after showers, and laundry dried with airflow rather than on radiators. If condensation is already a problem, see our cheap condensation fixes.
  • Let winter sun in. Open curtains on south-facing windows during the day for free solar gain, then close them before dusk to keep it.

Two popular tricks to skip

Cranking the thermostat up high does not heat the house any faster. The boiler runs at the same output either way; the dial only sets where it stops, so you just overshoot and waste gas. And leaving the heating on low all day is almost always dearer than heating only when you need it, because a warm house loses heat to the outside all day long. Both are covered in detail in our energy saving myths roundup.

Comfort is mostly about your skin, not the air five metres away, so spend pennies warming yourself before spending pounds warming the building. An electric blanket at 1.6p an hour, a heated throw at 3p, a hot water bottle at 4p a fill: any of these makes 18 to 19°C feel fine, and every degree you shave off the thermostat is worth roughly £70 a year on a typical gas bill.

Then make the cheap fixes permanent. Draught-proof the doors and letterbox, film the single glazing, block the unused chimney, close the curtains at dusk, bleed and unblock the radiators, and turn the boiler flow temperature down. Each one nudges the same number in the same direction, and together they are the difference between a house that feels cold at 20°C and one that feels comfortable at 18°C.

Tackle them in one go using our winter energy checklist, which puts every job in this guide into a single weekend-sized list.

faq.q: Is an electric blanket cheaper than turning the heating up?
faq.a: Massively. A 60 watt electric blanket costs about 1.6p an hour at 26.11p per kWh, so a whole winter of evening use comes to a few pounds. Raising the thermostat by one degree adds roughly £70 a year to a typical gas heating bill, so swapping a degree for a blanket pays for the blanket several times over in the first winter.

faq.q: What temperature is safe to keep the house at?
faq.a: Health bodies generally recommend at least 18°C in rooms you spend time in, with a little more for elderly people, babies and anyone with health conditions. The savings in this guide come from making 18 to 19°C feel comfortable, not from letting the house drop below safe levels, which risks damp, mould and health problems that cost far more than the gas saved.

faq.q: Does turning the thermostat up high heat the house faster?
faq.a: No. A standard boiler delivers heat at the same rate regardless of the thermostat setting; the dial only decides when the heating switches off. Setting 25°C to warm up faster just means the house overshoots the temperature you actually wanted and you pay for the extra gas.

faq.q: What is the cheapest way to keep one person warm in the evening?
faq.a: A heated throw or electric blanket plus decent layers. At current rates a five hour evening under a 120 watt heated throw costs about 16p, against £2.61 for a 2kW fan heater and more still for running the central heating warmer. A hot water bottle is even cheaper at around 4p a fill if you would rather avoid another plugged-in device.

faq.q: Do radiator reflector panels actually work?
faq.a: Yes, but only behind radiators on external walls, where they reflect heat back into the room that would otherwise soak into the wall. They cost a few pounds per radiator and fit without removing the radiator. On internal walls they achieve almost nothing, since heat going into an internal wall stays inside the house anyway.
